rules.label: Wizard's third rule
rules.text: Change the words. Change the world.

rules.label: Wizard's sixth rule
rules.text: Your spells may gain a life of their own.

rules.label: Wizard's eighth rule
rules.text: Everything gets a name. It's better if you know it.

rules.label: Wizard's ninth rule
rules.text: Assumptions are threads. Cut the threads.

rules.label: Wizard's eleventh rule
rules.text: You will be wrong. Try not to make it hurt.

anchors.label: This is not a programming book
There are a lot of books about programming. Many of them are called some variant of Programming for Dummies. This is not
This book does not teach you how to write software. It doesn't even tell you what kind of software to write. Instead, I
That is what a wizard would do.
I'm using the term wizard a lot, and I don't mean it in a way that suggests some level of competence or earned wisdom. I
But to understand where and how to change the world, you must first understand how it got to be the way it is. And the i
That is the point of this book.
Now, this doesn't mean we should just go about changing anything we like. For one thing, you will find it difficult to c
And the best way to do that is to look at what other wizards have done, and are doing.
Then steal their wisdom.
Most of the chapters in this book are stories about people doing exactly that. They changed the notation. They changed t
And after that, everyone else said the new thing was obvious.
That is how magic usually works.
One place worth stealing from is the Web. It's one of the very few examples of a software system so successful that it t
The Web is a chaotic place, full of shining new buildings, patched machinery, forgotten corridors, and ruins that still
Let's find out.
Numbers: bigger than you think
How do you make a number bigger than your fingers?
That sounds like a childish question, but it is one of the first great programming questions. You have a thing in the wo
Numbers are a good place to begin because they look natural. They are not. Number systems are inventions. Interfaces. Li
We are going to follow one idea as it gets stolen, bent, and improved: each new number system hides a little more work i
The first interface
It's no secret that programming is based on numbers. Two of them: 0 and 1.
It may come as a surprise that one of those numbers is much newer than the other. But before we get to zero, or binary,
Hands.
At first, the best number system was the one attached to your body.
That's not a joke. Fingers are portable, visible, hard to lose, and conveniently arranged in groups. For small numbers,
You can count to 10 using two hands. But what if you want to add numbers to your cave painting? Well, you make tally mar
So the first number we used was 1, or the tally mark: I. But tally marks get cumbersome quickly. So someone invented a n
And so it stayed for quite a while. Until a Roman (or probably someone else, and the Romans just stole the idea) had the
What the Romans invented here was that you could write a calculation to describe the number. 4 is written as V minus I.
The simple rule is that smaller marks before larger marks mean subtraction. If they are placed after larger marks, they
So as an example, what does the Roman numeral MMXXI translate to in our Arabic numerals? Yes, we call them Arabic numera
Now what about MCMXCVIII? And we're still just around 2000. Roman numerals still get unwieldy fairly quickly. But it's b
Notice we've come from the age of cave paintings, at least 35,000 years ago, all the way to the Romans, and we still hav
But let's step back a bit and introduce the abacus. This simple device's origins are unknown. It may have been invented
By now, you may have noticed a pattern.
Everyone is stealing from everyone else.
That sounds worse than it is. Good ideas travel. They are copied, renamed, misunderstood, improved, translated, simplifi
Wizards don't insist on inventing everything themselves. They notice working magic, learn why it works, and carry it som
Wizard's first rule
Anyway, back to the abacus. Roman numerals hide small calculations in the order of symbols. The abacus does something mo
This is a Chinese version; you could probably find something like it still in use around the world today. The idea is si
But much more important is that here, again, the system introduces a calculation into the representation of numbers. It'
It's a lot easier to explain if I use Arabic numerals. They use the exact same multiplication by column as the abacus do
And here, for the first time in our story, we have the number 0. Without it, Arabic numerals are impossible. Positional
As a side note, the idea of a number 0 is much older than this. But its status was not well established. The mathematici
As another aside, I should probably call Arabic numerals Hindu numerals instead. It seems the Arabs got the idea from In
Now back to large numbers. By using position to multiply each numeral, we can write very large numbers. Much larger than
But there is another calculation hidden inside these numbers. This is called modular arithmetic. The idea is that we can
Why is this important? Well, this insight opens the way for mechanical calculators: devices that can add and subtract nu
Blaise Pascal is credited with making one of the first usable mechanical calculators. It could add and subtract, and wit
This is not just a history of numbers. It is a history of moving work around: from fingers, to marks, to positions, to m
I should mention the Antikythera device. This is a device from the classical Greek era, which is thought to have been ab
To really understand how much of a leap this is, just take a look back at our timeline of numbers. Around 100 BC is befo
We're getting pretty good at writing large numbers. We can write down 1,000,000 and read it without blinking an eye. But
To write that number down, you will need a number with at least 111 zeros. Clearly that is no longer practical. So enter
math block
But we can also use these to explain the positional nature of Arabic numerals:
table
So the number 972 is equal to $$ 9 \times 10^2 + 7 \times 10^1 + 2 \times 10^0 $$
So what about our original two numbers, 1 and 0? Well, if you understand the magic of positional notation, it turns out
Once you see that the base is just another design choice, decimal starts to look less like nature and more like a very s
You can use any number of numerals, such as 16 for hexadecimal numbers, for example:
math block
0x is just a convention to say 'this is a hexadecimal number', and since there are 16 single-position numerals, we've go
Or octal, with 8 numerals per position:
math block
Or minimize it to two numbers, 1 and 0, and you get binary:
math block
And using exponents, we can explain it a bit better:
table
This means that the binary number 101 is equal to
math block
This simplifies to
math block
Binary numbers are so ubiquitous in programming that you'll see the powers of 2 often. Here are the first few:
table
This is a good time to end our archaeological dig through numbers. There is quite a bit more to explore, but you can do
The important thing for now is this: every number system we saw did work for us. Tally marks counted. Roman numerals emb
So how do you make a number bigger than your fingers?
You stop trying to make your fingers bigger. You make the notation smarter.
A notation is not just a way to write something down. A good notation performs part of the work for you. If you like thi
Logic: the truth is out there
In the last chapter, we saw that a good notation can do some of the calculation for you. Logic does something similar fo
This matters because your brain is a wonderful machine, but it cheats. It guesses. It skips steps. It sees patterns that
You are standing before two doors. One door leads to your desire. The other leads to your doom. Before the doors are two
You may ask one question.
Obviously, you need to ask a question that reveals which door to use. But the obvious question doesn't work.
If you ask the truthful guard, she'll point to your desire. If you ask the lying guard, she'll point to your doom. Since
The problem isn't that you're not clever enough. The problem is that the truth has the wrong shape. So we need to put it
If you ask a guard directly which door leads to your desire, the table looks like this:
table
That doesn't help. The answer depends on which guard you asked, and you don't know that. So the famous solution is to as
Now whatever answer you get, you choose the other door.
If the guard you ask tells the truth, she must give you the wrong answer, because that is what the liar would say. If th
table
No matter which guard you ask, you get the wrong door. So you choose the other one.
The trick isn't that you became smarter than the guards. It's that you asked a question that made both possible worlds c
Wizard's second rule
Boolean logic
Today we call this Boolean logic, after George Boole. The tables above are called truth tables.
Boolean logic is brutally small. Everything gets squeezed into two values:
table
That should feel familiar. In the last chapter, we squeezed numbers into symbols and columns. Here we squeeze reasoning
And once reasoning fits into cases, we can start building machinery around it.
Boolean logic is at the core of modern programming. It is at the core of computers. Everything your computer does for yo
Most Boolean operations combine two terms. Let's call them p and q. Just like our guards, we don't know whether they are
The operations are: and, or and not.
table
table
And of course:
table
Combining these, we can also create nor and nand.
table
table
Wait a minute... what is this nand thing? Why not just write not and?
Well... it so happens that your computer can implement everything with just nand. All other operations can be written as
This is the second great trick in this chapter. First we turned a riddle into a table. Then we turned the table into a m
Tables don't lie, but people do. And we like it. So much that we've invented something entirely new. Something clever an
Language: the oldest trick
Previously we've discussed numbers and logic. But not language. Which is strange, because language has been doing all th
Language is magic. Old magic.
We're so used to it that we don't see it, but think about it. While you are reading this, I have the capability to injec
We don't know exactly when humans started to use language. But we can be sure it was before we started to write. The cap
The one constant in our use of language seems to be that every generation mutates the language they use. There is no hum
An interesting question related to this is usually discussed as linguistic relativity: does language dictate what we can
There is an actual wizard who has thought about this.
Alan Moore, who writes books and comics and also calls himself a magician, has a useful idea of magic. Not stage magic.
In that view, there is the physical world, made of bodies and stones and rain and hunger. And then there is the imagined
Language is one of the most direct ways to work in that imagined world. A story can make a monster. A treaty can make a
This is why language is magic in a very practical sense. It changes the imagined world, and the imagined world keeps rea
Computer programs make this even stranger. A program starts as an imagined structure: names, rules, conditions, promises
So when programmers choose words, they are not decorating the code. They are shaping the imagined machine that other peo
Programmers have their own version of this magic.
Guy Steele gave a talk called Growing a Language, in which he started with an absurdly small language and then slowly gr
That is the point.
A thought can be simple in your mind and expensive in your language. If the language has no word for the thing you mean,
That is why naming is not decoration. When you add the right word, you don't just shorten the sentence. You change what
Whatever the answer, language is not just a way to communicate ideas. Language is part of how ideas become possible to c
The words people already use decide what feels natural, what feels strange, what feels possible, and what feels like non
Wizard's third rule
This matters when you talk to other people. It will matter later when we talk about programming languages as well. Code
But before we get there, we need to look at the Web. Because the Web is one of the great examples of what happens when a
The Web: one string to rule them all
In 1989 a wizard called Tim invented the World Wide Web and changed the world forever.
That sounds dramatic, but 1989 was not exactly short on drama. Walls were falling. Regimes were cracking. The Cold War w
That is one of the problems with good magic. When it first appears, it often looks like paperwork.
The first website did not look like the future either. It did not have video, rounded corners, infinite scrolling, cooki
html block
It does not look revolutionary today. It barely even looks designed. But there is a little piece of that first page that
html block
The a tag is tiny. Almost suspiciously tiny. Just one letter. Hidden inside it is another invention, probably the more i
url block
The URL. The Uniform Resource Locator.
A spell for pointing anywhere.
Hypertext was already in the air
To understand why the URL mattered, we need to go back a little further than 1989.
The idea of hypertext was older than the Web. Ted Nelson coined the term in 1965, and you can trace the dream further ba
But there is a large gap between dreaming about a global web of knowledge and getting one that ordinary people can actua
Ted Nelson started the Xanadu project in the 1960s. It was ambitious, beautiful, and, as ambitious beautiful things ofte
HyperCard was wonderful inside its own little universe. A card could point to another card. A button could take you some
The awkward part was the boundary. What if the thing you wanted to point to was maintained by someone else? What if it l
Xanadu tried to design a grand hypertext universe. HyperCard made a smaller universe that people could actually play wit
That is the part I find interesting. Tim Berners-Lee did not have to invent networking from scratch. TCP/IP was there. H
The missing piece was a simple way for one document to say:
Over there on that machine. Over there using that protocol. Over there at that path.
A normal-looking address. With a lot of stolen machinery hiding inside it.
Three stolen pieces
Look again at the URL:
url block
It has three obvious parts:
text block
Then it combines them in one string that binds them all together.
The protocol tells your computer how to talk.
The hostname tells it which name in the global naming system to resolve.
The path tells the other side what you are looking for.
Put those pieces together, and a plain text document can point to a published resource on another computer, maintained b
That is a ridiculous amount of power for one string.
And the string is still readable. This matters. It matters more than programmers sometimes admit. You can copy a URL int
The URL is an address, but it is also a tiny user interface to the network.
Wizard's fourth rule
The path: Unix hiding in plain sight
Start with the path:
text block
If you have used a Unix-like system, this shape is familiar. A slash separates folders and files. A path starts at a roo
text block
This feels obvious now, but it was only one possible way to name files. DOS and Windows used backslashes and drive lette
text block
The URL chose the simpler Unix-shaped world.
Unix itself had stolen and simplified ideas from earlier systems such as Multics. One of its beautiful tricks is that us
That idea survives inside the URL. The path says:
text block
It does not mention a disk, a controller, a cabinet, a room, a CPU architecture or a filesystem implementation. All of t
A path is a useful lie. Like many useful lies in programming, it hides the machinery that would only get in the way.
Of course, useful lies still need accounting departments. Real names contain spaces, punctuation, accents, and all the o
That is not the glamorous part of the Web. It is the kind of small compromise that lets a simple text format survive the
The hostname: a global phone book that is not a book
Now look at the middle part:
url block
The double slash says that what follows is a network location. This convention was borrowed too; Berners-Lee has said he
Again, this is how wizards really work most of the time. They do not produce polished inventions from a vacuum. They not
The more important borrowed system here is DNS, the Domain Name System.
Before DNS, the internet was small enough that people could maintain host files: lists of names and IP addresses. Your c
DNS turned naming into a distributed system. One machine no longer needed a complete list of every other machine. Names
So info.cern.ch is doing more than naming a server. It hooks the URL into a shared global act of naming.
The URL did not solve naming. It gave DNS a place inside the hypertext spell.
The protocol: a slightly mad opening move
Finally, the first part:
url block
At first this seems like the boring bit. It tells the browser to use HTTP. Sensible enough.
But putting the scheme at the front was also a wonderfully strange move, because it left the door open for the link to e
Compare it with Gopher, another worldwide information system that appeared around the same time. For a brief moment, Gop
text block
A line like that can point to information on another Gopher server. The format is useful, but the world it imagines is s
A URL begins by saying which kind of space you are entering. http: is one possibility. A browser might also understand f
Some of these are network protocols. Some are browser instructions. Some are hooks into other applications. Some feel li
This is one of the reasons URL-shaped strings escaped the Web. APIs use them. Package managers use them. Apps use them.
That is usually a good sign. A design has become part of the language when people start using it in places its inventor
The small turn
So the URL is not impressive because every part of it was new. It is impressive because a few existing parts were turned
text block
That little shape was simple enough to write by hand, simple enough to paste into another document, and open enough that
This is where the URL feels most wizard-like to me. Xanadu tried to design the whole dream of hypertext. The URL aimed a
But the shape of the problem had changed. A document no longer had to contain the world it wanted to point into. It only
The first version of the Web could be poor, ugly, incomplete, and still useful. Perhaps especially because it was incomp
Why this matters for programming
Programmers often look for the important abstraction in the wrong place. We expect it to be a clever class hierarchy, a
Sometimes it is. Quite often it is just a string with a few separators in the right places.
The URL works because it crosses boundaries without swallowing the systems on either side. The document can contain a li
Each part keeps its own job. The connection between them stays small.
That is much harder to design than it looks. Large systems keep tempting us to make larger abstractions: one model to ex
This is something a wizard is very aware of. A small change in representation can make a problem manageable. Sometimes i
The URL gave the Web a way to point at documents. Now the Web needed a way to give those documents shape.
The Web: the shape of words
A URL can point to a document. But pointing is not enough. Once the browser arrives, it still has to know what it is loo
What shape should a document have?
The question sounds harmless. A document is just text, right? Perhaps text with some headings, links, emphasis, images,
HTML chose a tree.
That choice made the Web easy enough to write by hand, easy enough for browsers to recover from mistakes, and structured
As I wrote in the previous chapter, in 1989 a wizard called Tim invented the World-Wide Web and changed the world foreve
To understand an invention, and the problem it solves, you need to understand its history. I've already talked about Hyp
Markup is simply an additional marking on a document, added to add extra meaning. The general idea is now called a marku
The editor added markup to tell the typesetter what font and font size to use, as well as other layout decisions.
When the first computers came around, they were very limited in the kind of output they could create. If there was a pri
Once the output devices gained more capabilities, their manufacturers quickly realized a need to somehow instruct these
The biggest problem was how to create better printed output. Early on, the only way to get decent printed output, was to
The wizards at Bell Labs, who were busy inventing Unix, were not impressed. They wanted their Unix and C manuals to be n
Another wizard, called Knuth, wanted to write a book about programming. Well actually, he wanted to write the book on pr
So in 1974 he took some time off to develop his own, better, typesetting system. That hiatus turned out to be 11 years a
Both troff and TeX use markup languages specifically to typeset documents before putting them on paper. As such they are
This is where things get interesting. Sometime in the 1970's some wizards from IBM created a system called GML. This eit
gml block
Unfortunately their work only made it out of IBM as a product you could buy and use, somewhere at the end of the 1980's.
GML eventually turned into SGML--Standard Generalized Markup Language, which became an industry standard in 1986. And in
Here is that first HTML page again:
html block
This is not an SGML document, it doesn't have a DTD header. Later HTML would become compliant with SGML, at least until
It does use the same syntax to differentiate between markup and content. All markup tags are enclosed in characters.
Let's take a closer look at the reasons behind this format. One of the main goals of the web was to make it easy for peo
This means that the source code should be human readable. So, to make the structure more clear, a markup had a start and
code block
This is technically feasible, maybe even simpler. However, for a human this is much more difficult to read. And cruciall
Another consequence of the chosen format is that some characters can not be used in the content anymore. Clearly the and
However, now we've added the & character to the list of special characters as well. This is easily solved:
And these are the basics of HTML.
Now for the tricky parts. As you've seen, HTML has elements that have a start and end tag. This means you can enclose ot
html block
Instead, you are supposed to write this:
html block
And this HTML can be represented as a tree structure:
This is how your web browser understands this HTML. Most modern browsers allow you to take a look at this structure, by
The HTML specification forces you to create a tree structure of your document. There can be no overlapping markup.
Wizard's fifth rule
This tree structure does have benefits. You can also write the above HTML as follows:
html block
And it would render the same. The tree structure is now made visible by the level of indenting in the source. This is no
To make this work, web browsers apply what is called white-space collapsing. In general, any time you add multiple white
For example, both lines in the example below will line out perfectly:
html block
Even the extra 3 spaces at the start of the second line do not show up.
Now if you really want to open a can of worms, try writing a rich text editor, with full WYSIWYG (What-You-See-Is-What-Y
html block
However, what you are seeing is an editor, designed by Microsoft originally for Internet Explorer 5.5, with an API that
The only successful in-browser editors, that are capable of editing any HTML and deliver a good end result, such as clea
This is a good lesson to learn: All choices have consequences. The less choices you make, the less consequences will hau
The Web: waking up the words
HTML gave Web documents a shape. JavaScript gave them behavior. But it also left us with a new question:
What happens when a prototype becomes the platform?
This chapter is about JavaScript, but it is also about a familiar software story. A demo is created in a hurry. It works
That is both terrifying and, sometimes, lucky.
JavaScript was made by a wizard called Brendan Eich. And its creation story is almost mythical.
JavaScript
Lets go back a little bit, before may 1995. You are a young developer tasked with creating an interactive experience on
The only way would be to add a form. A visitor of your website would fill it in, then press submit. The form data is sen
In 1995 Netscape was king of the webbrowser hill. Microsoft Internet Explorer 1.0 would be released in august 1995, and
Obviously for this to come to fruition, the web would need to be more interactive, more programmable. So Netscape set ou
Why Netscape chose Java isn't hard to see. At the time there will still many different computer architectures in use. Wr
Enter Brendan Eich -- "At least client engineering management including Tom Paquin, Michael Toy, and Rick Schell, along
How short order? How about 10 days (and probably nights.)
Surely that was only a proof-of-concept, a demo? Well, yes, but this demo, this prototype ended up in production, virtua
Wizard's sixth rule
However, we got uncharacteristically lucky. JavaScript was not a tuned-down, dummified version of Java. You see, Brendan
Scheme is a descendant of Lisp (and Algol.) It is nothing like Java. But as any Lisp-like language, you can change the l
As any prototype that is pushed into production too soon, JavaScript has its problems. But its origins in Scheme, and Se
In 2008 JavaScript got some positive publicity, with a book by wizard Douglas Crockford -- "Javascript: The Good Parts".
Toil and Trouble
Today it is hard not to associate the DOM (Document Object Model) with JavaScript. And that is not fair to JavaScript. T
But the DOM is still considered a compilation target. Not something that you program directly, unless you are a masochis
All this stuff makes web development so much harder than it needed to be. This is the real fallout from rushing JavaScri
A simple example is the JavaScript module system. For the longest time it didn't exist. In the browser you could mimic i
And even though there now is a clear standard module system, what we end up using is often an incompatible version of it
The browser-shaped computer
Netscape's dream did not quite happen in the way Netscape imagined. We did not all simply stop using operating systems a
But something close enough happened to be worth noticing.
A lot of what people now call "using the computer" is really using the browser. Mail, documents, calendars, maps, bankin
ChromeOS makes that idea unusually explicit. It is an operating system built around the assumption that the web is not j
Electron approaches the same idea from the other direction. If people still expect desktop applications, fine. Put a bro
Progressive Web Apps sit somewhere in the middle. They try to let web applications behave a bit more like installed soft
None of these are the final answer. They are clues.
The browser began as a way to read linked documents. JavaScript turned it into a programmable surface. Over time that su
The network starts to look less like something the computer connects to, and more like the place where the computer happ
That raises a question this chapter does not answer yet. If the web becomes the operating system, who owns the home dire
For now, we pick up where we left off in chapter 4, languages. This time we'll teach the computer how to lie.
Teaching machines our words
A few chapters ago we looked at language as magic. Human language does not merely carry thoughts around; it changes what
Programming languages do the same thing, but with less tolerance for ambiguity and more semicolons.
This chapter returns to that idea, but now turns it toward programming proper. A programming language is not primarily a
Computer languages
A common misconception is that programming languages are designed to allow you to program computers. If that were the on
code block
This machine code is written in octal, to make it slightly more readable for humans. But the first really usable step is
code block
And now with some labels and comments:
code block
This is the code to make a PDP-11 computer boot. It is commonly known as bootstrap code.
Even in this enhanced, more human form, this code is not very readable. Or expressive. Most humans don't code in assembl
That is why there are so many programming languages. Different humans need different thoughts to be cheap.
COBOL was designed to make business programs look more like business language. Its intended use was in offices, banks, g
cobol block
Believe it or not, COBOL is still in use today. The code above is copied from a current manual for DB2, an IBM database
That is not an accident. Business software tends to outlive the people who wrote it. Once a language becomes part of the
Fortran made a different bet. It was built for scientists and engineers. It made formulas, arrays, loops, and numerical
fortran block
COBOL and Fortran are not just old languages. They are old answers to different questions. What should business code loo
Finally we arrive at Lisp. A wizard called John McCarthy dreamed up Lisp between 1956 and 1958. Lisp made a stranger bet
That sounds like a technical detail until you feel what it does. Lisp doesn't just make it possible to solve problems. I
Here is a tiny example:
lisp block
This adds a new word to the language: unless. After that, programmers can write code in terms of the idea they mean, ins
That is why Lisp keeps returning in conversations about programming languages, not because everyone should use it, or be
A Lisp interpreter can be written in Lisp itself. Paul Graham shows one in Roots of Lisp, based on McCarthy's original.
Differences, commonalities, and why we can't have nice things
Computer languages are more commonly known as programming languages. They are designed to make a computer do something.
Programming languages are designed, unlike human languages. However, they aren't designed to make a computer do anything
Just like human languages, programming languages make it easier for some ideas to be expressed than others. The best pro
Unfortunately, that's also not true.
The problem is communication. Not between the human wizard and the computer. The problem is communicating ideas between
Wizard's seventh rule
Reading code is hard. It is much easier to write code. This sounds nonsensical, yet it is true. It is the source of the
One answer is to make programming languages bigger. Add more standard stuff. Add design patterns. Add frameworks. Make s
There is a reason this keeps happening. Shared shapes make code easier to read. If every project invents its own private
But there is a cost. Once the shared language becomes too large, the real problem starts to disappear underneath the sta
So the natural pressure on programming languages is strange. Small, powerful languages are wonderful for the writer, but
Just like human languages.
A wizard should be aware of this tension. You do not escape it by choosing the one true language, because the one true l
That is where domain-specific languages become interesting.
A DSL is not automatically a separate language with a parser and a manual. Sometimes it is. SQL is its own world, and fo
But before we build one, we need to notice something stranger: every program already contains a language of its own.
Every program grows a language
A program is not only written in a language. A program also creates a language.
Every variable name, function name, class name, method name, command name, file name, and folder name adds a word to tha
This is why reading code is hard. It is much easier to write code. When you write code, you already know the language yo
Most of the time, this local language grows by accident. That is the uncomfortable part. You do not get to choose whethe
You add a function because one bit of code got annoying. You add a class because three things started to look alike. You
That is not automatically bad. It is how useful code grows.
But sometimes the local language wants to stop being accidental. Sometimes the problem has a vocabulary of its own, and
At that point, a wizard may decide to make the language explicit.
Wizard's eighth rule
Domain Specific Languages
A Domain Specific Language, or DSL, is a language made for one kind of problem. A DSL is much more limited than a normal
When you start using DSLs, you don't use a single programming language anymore. You use many small languages, and glue t
Creating a true DSL means creating your own compiler and runtime. It makes the boundary between the DSL and the source p
The best DSLs are so well known that we don't think of them as DSLs anymore.
One example is Regular Expressions. Any non-trivial program will have them. Beginning programmers hate them, because the
Another example is SQL. Almost any software that uses a database will have some SQL in it. It may be hidden behind an OR
Done correctly, a DSL allows you to divide your problem along natural boundaries in the domain. Then you can solve each

The knitted castle
Why is it so difficult to make reusable software?
Objects would do it. Components would do it. Packages would do it. Services would do it. Web components would do it. Fra
And then, a few years later, we discover that we have not built a Lego castle after all. We've made a tangle of yarn.
The famous wizard Alan Kay once said:
I think the situation is more dire than that. We're not stacking bricks. We're knitting castles.
A pyramid is at least made of separate stones. They may be heavy, badly placed, and difficult to move, but you can still
A knitted castle is different. Every part is made out of the same thread as every other part. The wall continues into th
This is funny when it is yarn.
It is less funny when it is your application.
The dream of Lego
The dream is obvious. We want software pieces that behave like Lego bricks.
A Lego brick does not care whether it is used in a castle, a spaceship, a bridge, or a thing a small child insists is de
That boringness is part of the magic.
A Lego brick is reusable because it makes very few assumptions about the thing you are building. It does not decide the
Most software components are not like that.
Most software components are more like a beautifully carved castle tower with a bit of wall still attached, some electri
You can reuse it. But now your project must have the right hill.
Every useful thing grows threads
When you first write a piece of software, it often starts clean. A function receives values and returns a value. A compo
Then the real world arrives.
The function needs configuration. The component needs styling. The module needs logging. The user must be allowed to can
None of these changes are absurd. That is the annoying part.
A knitted castle is usually not built by foolish decisions. It is built by reasonable decisions made locally.
One thread for the user. One thread for the database. One thread for the framework. One thread for the CSS. One thread f
After a while, the component no longer has a simple shape. It has a climate.
To reuse it, you do not only need the component. You need the weather system it expects to live in.
Wizard's ninth rule
Packages are not the same as parts
JavaScript has one of the largest ecosystems of reusable packages ever created. npm is both a miracle and a warning labe
It proves that reuse is possible. You can install a parser, a date library, a test runner, a renderer, a bundler, a colo
But a package is not automatically a Lego brick.
A package is a unit of distribution. It says: here is a thing you can download and import.
That is not the same as saying: here is a thing whose assumptions fit naturally into your problem.
A package can be reusable as a whole and still be impossible to take apart. You may like its parser but not its data mod
This is where much of the promise quietly leaks away. We have become very good at shipping bundles of software around th
The piece crosses the network easily. The idea does not always cross the boundary.
Reuse has a direction
One reason the Lego dream fails is that we talk about reuse as if all reuse were the same.
It is not.
A pure function is often easy to reuse because it asks for little. Give it input. Receive output. It does not need to kn
A file format can be reusable because many programs can agree on it without sharing the same code.
A protocol can be reusable because different systems can meet at a boundary and then go their separate ways again.
A UI component is harder. It often wants data, styling, events, state, focus rules, accessibility behavior, theme decisi
An application feature is harder still. A shopping cart is not just a list of products. It touches pricing, stock, ident
At some point you are no longer reusing a part. You are importing a small civilization.
So let's rephrase the question. The useful question is not:
The useful question is:
Can the calculation be reused without the UI? Can the data format be reused without the database? Can the protocol be re
Don't ask for universal reuse first. Universal reuse is where many good designs go to become enormous.
A wizard looks for the boundary.
The boundary is the component
This is the part that is easy to miss.
The reusable thing is not only the code. It is the boundary around the code.
The boundary is where the bargain is made. This data may cross. These names matter. This part stays hidden. This part is
A bad boundary leaks the whole world.
A good boundary is boring in the right places.
This is why small libraries often age better than clever frameworks. A small library can sometimes say: give me this val
A framework often says: live here.
Living somewhere can be pleasant. A framework can give you roads, plumbing, electricity, building codes, a market square
This is also why standards matter. A standard creates a boundary that does not belong to one package. HTML, URLs, JSON,
The Web itself works because many different pieces agree just enough to meet each other.
Fighting for the Lego castle
You cannot defeat complexity once and for all. That is the sort of promise that sells books, frameworks, and conference
But you can fight.
You can keep asking what assumptions a piece is carrying. You can make dependencies explicit instead of letting them see
Keep the stable rules of a system away from the parts that talk to browsers, databases, networks, and users. Pass plain
Make small languages for the stable parts of the problem, as we did with JAQT. Not always new languages with parsers. So

The knitted castle is always waiting. Every feature wants to tie one more loop. The wizard's work is not to avoid all lo
Now let's talk about what happens when blocks start carrying little worlds of their own.

Being wrong without breaking everything
Software changes the world, and is changed by it.
Any useful program will eventually meet new machines, new uses, new libraries, new laws, new attacks, new expectations,
This is one of the fundamental differences between software and older engineering metaphors.

That is not a moral failure. It is what happens when you have to work before the future has finished introducing itself.

So the next question is not how to make a system clever enough to predict all of that.
It is how to make one open enough to benefit from work you did not control.
That is where the Web comes back into the story: not as something finished, but as shared ground we can still build on.

For that to work, the data needs to travel farther than the program.
Not all of it. Not always publicly. Not without permission. Not without care. But conceptually, the data must be able to
This is where linked data belongs in the story of the Web.
The URL made documents reachable.
HTML made documents understandable enough for browsers to render and programs to inspect.
JavaScript made documents programmable.
Linked data asks whether the things described by those documents can become reachable and understandable too.
If they can, software gets a different shape. Applications do not have to be kingdoms that contain all meaning. They can
That is the bridge to the next chapter.
If data can have addresses, if facts can link to other facts, and if meanings can be shared across boundaries, then the
where should a person's own data live?
The Web as home: who owns your home directory?
Chapter 7 left a question hanging.
If the web becomes the operating system, who owns the home directory?
The previous two chapters added pressure from two sides. If innovation happens elsewhere, users should be able to benefi
On a normal computer the home-directory question has an obvious answer, at least in theory. There is a place where your
You can install a different text editor without rewriting all your text files. You can open an image in another program.
The web grew up differently.
On the web, each application tends to bring its own little home with it. Your mail lives in the mail app. Your photos li
This is understandable. It is also strange.
The private castle is not only greed. It is also easy architecture. When an app owns the interface, the account, the dat
But the boundary is in the wrong place.
The app has become the container for the user's life.
Software moved into the network. The user's home did not really come with it.
Instead we got thousands of private little castles.
The old shape and the hidden shape
One way to describe the modern web is this:
text block
The application is the starting point. You choose an app, create an account inside it, and then your data begins to accu
There is another possible shape:
text block
The user is the starting point. The data belongs near the user. Applications become tools that are allowed to read, writ
That may look like a small rearrangement. It is not.
In the first shape, replacing software means moving your life from one castle to another. In the second shape, replacing
This does not make the problem easy. Some parts become harder.
Permissions can no longer be an afterthought. Shared vocabularies and data formats have to do real work. Applications ca
That difficulty is where the boundary becomes real. A boundary is not just a line in a diagram. It is the notation that
Small, replaceable software requires a place where the important things are not owned by the software.
A web with a home directory
There is a project called Solid that tries to explore this direction. I do not want to turn this chapter into a manual f
Solid asks what would happen if identity and data were separated from applications. A person has an identity on the web.
This sounds almost too obvious, once said aloud. Of course your contacts should be yours. Of course your calendar should
And yet the obvious thing is not how most of the web works. The current shape was easier to build. It was convenient for
This is where the earlier chapters start to matter. A URL is powerful because it lets worlds point at each other. HTML i
The personal web needs the same kind of thinking.
It is not enough to say that users should own their data. Ownership is an important word, but by itself it does not desi
You need boundaries that make replacement normal.
Small tools, not kingdoms
If data and identity can outlive applications, software can become smaller.
A notes app does not have to become a complete platform for every thought you will ever have. It can be a good editor fo
This is the Lego castle trying to escape from the knitted castle again.
Of course, it will not escape completely. Real software always grows threads. Applications need assumptions. Data shapes
But the direction matters.
A private app castle asks: how can we keep the user inside?
A personal web asks: how can this tool remain useful after the user replaces it?
That is a very different question. It points to different designs. It rewards different behavior. It makes different fut
And this is the theme that keeps returning throughout this book. The hard problem may not be the thing you first thought
Change where you are looking from, and the problem changes.
If you look from the app, everything else has to orbit the app. The data, the identity, the permissions, the habits, the
If the person has a home on the Web, applications can come and go. They can still matter. They can still be excellent. B
Epilogue: shifting ground
Most of this book was written from experience.
That sounds sturdier than it is.
Experience is not a foundation. It is a record of where the ground used to be solid.
For years, programming meant learning to see through the machine: the notation, the shape of the data, the hidden bounda
Those lessons still matter. I think they matter more than ever.
But while this book was being written, the craft moved. Machines became able to write code, explain code, invent code, a
Maybe the old lessons will become more important. Maybe some of them will become quaint. Maybe the next useful shape is
I do not know.
That is the final lesson.
Wizard's final rule
